global.title: Powerhouse Software Architecture

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: The foundational claim — document models are not just data containers but living entities that capture relationships, states, and operations, forming the backbone of every layer in the stack
Why document models matter for decentralized orgs — contributors work asynchronously on documents that move through states (draft, review, approved) without linear processes or central coordination
The structural principle — separating infrastructure, applications, and customization allows concerns to evolve independently and organizations to compose what they need
Layer 1 implementation — the Reactor is a storage node supporting local, cloud, and decentralized adapters (Ceramic, IPFS), with network components like event buses and load balancers for scale
Layer 2 design pattern — host apps like Connect and Fusion are empty shells that gain all functionality through plugins, enabling the same base to serve wildly different use cases
Layer 3 purpose — each organization deploys its own customized instances with bespoke Connect plugins, Switchboard APIs, and Fusion marketplaces built on the shared stack
Articulates the event-sourcing trade-off: audit trails guaranteed, but querying requires read models
Development workflow: always present model proposals before implementing — prevents rework
The 21-model ecosystem inventory organized into 5 zones — the concrete instantiation of the architecture
The three-layer architecture (infrastructure, host, customization) — the foundational structural pattern
Powerhouse operates at the intersection of software, legal, and OSS business models

global.description: Document models as core unit across three layers: Data Infrastructure (Reactor), Host Application (Connect/Switchboard/Fusion), Customization.
global.orientation: Powerhouse's software architecture is built around document models as the fundamental unit of data and workflow. Everything — storage, sync, editing, and customization — operates on documents. The architecture separates into three layers: the Data Infrastructure Layer (Reactor nodes with pluggable storage adapters), the Host Application Layer (Connect, Switchboard, Fusion, Renown, and Academy as plugin-driven shells), and the Customization Layer (organization-specific instances and plugins). This layered approach means contributors work with tailored tools while sharing common infrastructure. The Reactor handles sync and storage, host apps provide the UI shells, and plugins turn them into purpose-built platforms.
